item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"give her a normal number"

2442

42

Nice job, guys!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wouldn't have guessed the "twist" in a million years if it weren't for the spoilers. It's nice and all, but I'm not really feeling it anyways. An interesting posibility though, and well written. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea, but I think it should have been expanded on more. If I understand it correctly, the entire SCP foundation is a sort of coping mechanism for Stacie. Her personal demons are skips, other attempts at finding coping mechanisms which failed are the GoIs, SCP-2442 is her therapist. I think that's interesting, but the article puts off a lot of work making that connection off on the reader. As it is, it feels like I'm reading a good premise, but none of the implications have been developed.

There's nothing here to convince me that there is any reason to believe that SCP-2442 isn't delusional. The GoIs being failed attempts at coping mechanisms vaguely makes some sort of sense, but it's not expanded on at all. When I think about it in any amount of detail, it feels like a lazy dream-theory-esque explanation for the foundation. It would have done this article good if there was some sort of link between individual GoIs and the coping mechanisms actually being attempted. How does the Church of the Broken God equate to a coping mechanism? How does it relate to compartmentalization? How are most of the skips explained with this premise? Some skips are violent and dangerous, so there's a vague metaphorical connection to personal demons, but it's not a very strong connection, and there are many skips that defy that explanation totally. How does a toaster that makes others believe they are a toaster have anything to do with it? A machine that can produce a cup of anything? A skip which can't be talked about without mentioning that it used to be a library?

I really wanted to see some strong connection made that made me really think for a second that the entire foundation may just be a form of therapy for someone. I didn't get anything. The article seems to be played really straight too. I honestly don't see why an O5 would react with "No! It can't be!" considering there is no detail to the explanation that would give it any credibility. If one of the researchers got stoned and said, "Bruh, what if all the skips are someone's regrets and traumas?" I feel like it would have had as much impact. This skip is in desperate need of more material.

So, I hate that I didn't like this one. I wanted to like it. I feel like it's far from unsalvageable. I really think this could work. As it stands, though, it's a -1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thinking about it, this one really oversells how "dangerous" it is with the reaction of the O5's. What is the difference between a reality bender, and a being where reality exists entirely in their mind? I think it's entirely philosophical - from a practical perspective, for what it means for anyone dealing with them, there's no difference. Existence for everyone else is contingent on the whim of the powerful being.

As the foundation is used to dealing with reality benders, another one coming along that actually exhibits a relatively limited set of behaviors, primarily limited to making things disappear, doesn't seem worth the O5's reacting like it's any more serious of a threat than other reality benders. Even if this one behaves as if reality is all in their head. I definitely don't know why they'd choose to treat the claim as if things are actually that way.

I feel this entry would be better if it were written based on that viewpoint - that it's a reality bender that believes the world is only in their head. Perhaps keep with SCP-2442 as the reality bender himself, somehow deluded into thinking he's a therapist for that mysterious female entity, which he's actually just created himself.

As it is, thinking I'm going to have to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am gonna +1, because it's written — nah, no actually, I'm gonna no vote. There are just a couple of things with this one.

+ show block
– hide block

From a writing standpoint, it just… moves. Really fast. It goes too fast. I feel like there's not enough build up. That being said, I don't know what to add, but it feels like it gets to the dialogue with her way too fast. I don't think it would be any better if there was more information in the description that didn't add anything to the story, so I have to say that this might be an unsolvable problem, and a product of the idea more than the writing.

So, the second thing… No, there is no Foundation canon. But my headcanon does contain all of the SCPs in the same universe. They contradict each other? Woopsie dasies, looks like the O5 put false information somewhere in these to throw people off the trail. On the other hand, maybe all of what they have written is true, and its just another thing about the universe that we don't understand (like how they can exist in the same world).

So my interpretation of this, that lets it fit with the rest of the world… is that it doesn't work the way it says it does. It is an anomaly that does everything to lead you to this implication that the world of the SCP Foundation is just a construct of this woman's mind, but that's the point of the anomaly and not what's actually going on. There were no SCP-[DATA LOST]'s. They never existed, you just think they did and she got rid of them. For being a reality bender, I can say that maybe she can just pop things out of existence, like people or such. But to remain consistent, I'd say that there were no people she got rid of either, for my interpretation. In fact, I think this whole anomaly exists as a memetic, and not a reality bender. Unfortunately, no one can figure that out. Maybe some have theorized, but there is no one can prove anything.

For example, she gets rid of a person. That person actually never existed — not that she has retrocausal powers that wrote them out of existence, no, they literally never existed. This "memetic", however, does not spread by hearing a certain phrase or anything else. The information spreads to every being in the universe, and if they theoretically would have had contact with this person, this process would do this complicated farce of "they existed but they don't now".

Recordings and images of her will carry these strange images, but they aren't there, they are a spread of her memetic. By her being on that screen, you will think you see all these things that are part of her memetic, but they aren't actually there.

Actually, if this was all true, this would be the most difficult, possibly impossible anomalies to dethrone. It might not even be a memetic because of the way it works, and more an undefined "anomaly of the mind".

Okay, okay, I just talked out how to work this into my headcanon and… I can't help but like it now. Even the O5 Council is affected. And that's the scary part. The whole world can be easily convinced they are a construct of someone else's mind, and thus throw away everything they stand for and do for a reason that doesn't really exist. I love it. Okay, okay, you won. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very bold, rewriting the whole premise of Foundation - not even SCP-0001 can handle this much
And I'm not sure whether that is a good thing or bad thing
For me I found this 'unsettling'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea in a piece of fiction that everything is actually product of the narrator's imagination, but I think the SCP format is not the most ideal for that trope: it kind of rests on an unreliable narrator, which is hard to pull of as a scp, and indeed is not happening here, the closest thing to a narrator being the supposedly fictional O5s. The end result is that the explanation that the scp is just a delusional reality bender has more weight.
(I still liked this enough for an upvote, though.)

item.title: Re: Question
item.content:encoded: Not to necro-post, but given the therapy angle and the fact they vanish when brought to the thing's attention, it could be that as Stacie opens up about her inner demons (the scps), they no longer become issues for her (like taking a person with fear of heights climbing as experience-therapy).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Easily one of the most terrifying scips around, at least when you REALLY think about it. Damn right this gets a +1.

item.title: Re: [PALINDROME INTENSIFIES]
item.content:encoded:
Bro, your a genie. New fave SCP
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't like when St. Elsewhere pulled this trick and I don't find this to be any better. I believe this entire trope to be pretentious and intellectually unstimulating. The writing is solid but bland. The characters fulfilled their narrative roles but had no real spark of life. I find myself hard pressed to even remember one of their names despite having just finished reading. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the climax is spread a bit too thin, or maybe that the build to it is sort of unfocused. I think the problem I'm having is that what's going on is sort of muddled and confused right up until the last log makes it obvious. Prior to that, it seems like the SCP might just be a particularly charismatic reality bender. I think by the time I get to the last log, I should be wondering what this guy's deal is, or I should have a picture forming in my head; not necessarily the right one, but something that makes me want to get to that climax. As it is, I didn't really feel any reason to be invested in the story until the very end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would love this as a tale. As an SCP, I'm quite torn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really liked this one. Got it without the spoilers, and then had fun reading back through the article and interpreting it all in that light. Nice internal consistency. I'll agree that there could be more without it definitely harming it, but I also think it's fine as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, dropped out of chat due to faulty Internet connection, Soulless. Anyways, I wanted to reiterate: an excellent concept here, due to the very subtleties that go all through the article. The implications are well placed and I find zero troubles with the execution. Thus, upvoted.

Now, several people mention that this could be longer. I concur. This could ve longer. There could be mentions to ZK scenarios, plans to kill the skip (yeah I know, frown at me. But I can totally see Stacie having self-destructive thoughts and the O5 doing whatever it takes to protect their "Mission") and a connection with a person or persons outside the Foundation. Otherwise, great skip, great execution, excepcional concept.

Also, my personal take on the "turn it into a 001" business. Don't. The Foundation being fictional has been done, sure. But the problem is that this is far more scarier as an item that was found far, far later than the beginning of the Foundation, and is in no way central to the Foundation itself. It is actually peripheral because the O5s prefer it to be so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, that was a typo. Feexing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Swann's 001 proposal handles the reaction to the existential crisis of the Foundation not being real in a much less incongruous way than this.

In this article, the O5's are not people. The only people involved in this article are Stacie and her doctor. Everything are merely aspects of Stacie, except for the doctor, and everyone knows, though they may also not know. It is not the rational and the sane and the secure that seek such grand designs for the sake of feeling safe and normal…

I hope this makes a bit more sense, though I understand if you dislike it regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…usually though verbal indication.

Is this supposed to be, "through"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoting because I really hate the execution. Leaking information to a Keter skip that results in further containment breaching and the loss of an SCP object is not "non-vital" information, and having three O5s panicking at the end is just so very 2008, even if the spoilers arguably give a justification to it.

Swann's 001 proposal handles the reaction to the existential crisis of the Foundation not being real in a much less incongruous way than this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I concur with Kalinin. There isn't enough here right now to support such an expansive idea. It just didn't feel like there was enough focus on the concept for it to give the full impact.

I also found this to be just a tad too subtle for my taste, although that is a personal gripe.

item.title: Re: Question
item.content:encoded: The article is far more metaphorical than most articles. I am afraid I can't really explain in further detail than that- it's all one big metaphor for very human, mundane experiences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is one instance where more is definitely more. You can't understate the impact of this particular skip. Of course, I'm not sure if this works, on reflection, but I felt compelled to upvote regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll see about editing it some more to give more of a personality to Stacie. It's a bit difficult, because Stacie herself doesn't really want to face the truth or have an impact on her own life (thus why the doctor is tasked with finding and pointing them out, interacting with the people, etc.)

I can try to add one or two more excerpts to portray further, as I had slimmed them down previously for fear of this article being too long. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't actively dislike it, and I was able to surmise what you were up to without the spoilers. I don't want to go so far as to say that it should be reworked as an 001 Proposal; there have been previous skips where that was the basis for downvotes, and those comments rubbed me the wrong way something fierce. I do think, though, that for something that is addressing the larger implications of the semi-shared universe that we all write in, it needs a little more heft to it.

For something that's oriented on particular people and personalities, I feel like I know very little about the person in whose mind the Foundation exists. The kinds of things that inhabit this universe being the product of a single person's psychological abnormalities…that person must have some fairly profound issues and traumatic experiences. But I don't really get much of a sense of why exactly things have turned out this way for your pseudo-protagonist. We all live in the character's head, but there's almost no character there.

I don't object to this living on the mainlist, per se, but if it's going to be there, I think that there needs to be a little more. Not in the "I need backstory(tm) and Covenient Notes From Vanished Skipmakers" sense, but some more shaping and outlines of what exactly is lurking behind the curtain here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My impression was that she either helped create or influenced the Foundation, not just some coping fantasy with her head. It's mostly up to headcanon really, so make her influence how you want it to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this was a tale I'd upvote, but even still I like the idea and the execution is about as good as it can be in this format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm the opposite of Thekillerax; I love the idea and am a sucker for head games, but I disliked the execution here

item.title: Question
item.content:encoded:
I'm a bit confused. What do all these random SCP's she's getting rid of have do with her life?

Did she meet them in "somewhere" and have a bad experience with them, from which her life is utterly crushed and from there realized she had to move on and manifested SCP-2442 and helped/influenced the creation of the SCP Foundation to help rally here bad experiences with sad entities from "somewhere" into one place where she can deal with them?

Please tell me if have this wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Normally I HATE the whole " BLAH BLAH is not real, it's just in BLAH BLAH's head!" trope, but it was handled deftly and executed beautifully here. While it conflicts with my personal headcanon, I believe it deserves a hearty +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, it should be a 001 proposal in its implications, but that would also mean the O5 would be willingly acknowledging it as something larger than they are willing to. So instead they give it a 'regular' number and try to threaten anyone that tries to dig too deep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't we already have a "scp is not real" Scip?
Anyway, the tone, grammar and stuff are well done, but idea that the Foundation only existing in a girl's mind really doesn't sit well with me. Neutral vote, as for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does it occur to anyone else that this can be reworked into a 001 proposal?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral voting this one. It's well-written, but it screws with my personal headcanon a bit too much.

item.title: Re: [PALINDROME INTENSIFIES]
item.content:encoded: Ha! I knew it!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's very roundabout and obtuse. I like what you were aiming for, but I didn't understand it until reading your spoilers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The real problem for me is that the implications it leaves are the hook, but you bend the thing flat by just saying "Remember all those interesting questions you had about what the hell happened here? Well forget it, metaclorians. Yep. No interesting mystery to see here folks."

It's alright if you disagree, but in order for me to like this article, I have to pretend the spoilers you added don't exist. That's just my personal opinion so take it how you will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah I got that, that's OK. Thank you for your feedback it is much appreciated. Unfortunately I don't think I can change too much to make it more pleasing to you, for this I do apologize and strive to provide a less un-magicking one for you next time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the Men in Black trilogy of movies, they say that the universe they inhabit is utterly inconsequential through some reveal at the end similar to what you said the Foundation basically is according to your spoilers.

For example, at the end of I think the second one, Agent K showed Agent J that the universe they inhabit is contained in a locker inside of a subway station of a larger universe, basically rendering their existence insignificant. However they played it off for laughs and weren't seriously saying that the world they live in and the organization they work for are meaningless.

And the metaclorians bit comes from the first star wars prequel in which they suck all the magic out of the force, this previously mystical thing which apparently tied all life together in the galaxy and turned it into just symbiotic bacteria in the bloodstream.

In summation, you saying that were all in her head kind of ruins everything cool about the Foundation universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry I'm unfamiliar with movies. What do you mean by pulling a "Men in Black"? Thank you for the response.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought it was interesting until you pulled a Men in Black with those spoilers of yours. Now it just seems weak and sucks all the magic out of it like metaclorians did to the force.

neutral vote without the spoilers.

item.title: [PALINDROME INTENSIFIES]
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers
Hide Spoilers

The SCP Foundation is a mental construct in a girl named Stacie's mind. The SCP Foundation represents Stacie's bad coping mechanism when dealing with her life, compartmentalizing everything, unable to let go or move on. SCP-2442 is her therapist, helping her locate her own inner demons to come to peace with them. The O5s are Stacie's darkest, most deep insecurities.

There are a few references in here. I hope this goes over well, it's my biggest one (implication wise) ever. Go figure it's my first Keter.
